stem: Let \( f(z) = ([[a2]])z^{2} + ([[a1]])z + ([[a0]]) \), a polynomial (hence entire), and let \( C \) be the positively oriented circle \( |z| = [[R]] \). Compute the closed contour integral \( \oint_{C} f(z)\,dz \).
solution: \( f \) is a polynomial, so it is analytic everywhere inside and on \( C \). By the Cauchy-Goursat theorem the integral of an analytic function around a closed contour is \( \oint_{C} f(z)\,dz = 0 \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( 2\pi i \), the value of \( \oint dz/z \); but \( f \) here has no singularity inside \( C \).
This is \( -2\pi i \); Cauchy-Goursat gives exactly 0 for an analytic integrand.
This treats the integral as \( \pi i \); an entire integrand contributes 0.
This treats the integral as \( -\pi i \); an entire integrand contributes 0.
This applies a residue-style factor to the constant term, but there is no pole to enclose.
This applies a residue-style factor to the leading coefficient, but there is no pole to enclose.

stem: Let \( f(z) = \dfrac{([[b1]])z + ([[b0]])}{(z-([[p]]))(z-([[q]]))} \), and let \( C \) be the positively oriented circle \( |z| = [[R]] \). Since both \( z=[[p]] \) and \( z=[[q]] \) lie outside \( C \), \( f \) is analytic on and inside \( C \). Compute \( \oint_{C} f(z)\,dz \).
solution: Both singularities of \( f \) lie outside \( C \), so \( f \) is analytic everywhere inside and on \( C \). By the Cauchy-Goursat theorem, \( \oint_{C} f(z)\,dz = 0 \), regardless of the values of the poles themselves.
distractor_rationales: This treats the boundary as if it enclosed \( 1/(z) \)-type singularity; no pole lies inside \( C \) here.
This is the negative of the same mistaken residue-style value.
This treats the integral as \( \pi i \), but an integrand analytic inside \( C \) contributes 0.
This mistakenly applies a residue formula at \( z=[[p]] \), which is not enclosed by \( C \).
This mistakenly applies a residue formula at \( z=[[q]] \), which is not enclosed by \( C \).
This evaluates the numerator's coefficients as if they were the answer, ignoring the theorem entirely.

stem: Let \( g(z) = ([[m]])e^{z} \), which is entire, and let \( C \) be the positively oriented circle \( |z| = [[R]] \). Compute the closed contour integral \( \oint_{C} g(z)\,dz \).
solution: \( g(z) = ([[m]])e^{z} \) is entire (analytic on all of \( \mathbb{C} \)), so it has no singularities inside or on \( C \). By the Cauchy-Goursat theorem, \( \oint_{C} g(z)\,dz = 0 \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly applies a residue-style factor of \( 2\pi i \) to the coefficient, but \( g \) has no pole inside \( C \).
This is the negative of the same mistaken residue-style computation.
This mistakenly evaluates \( g \) at a point and scales by \( 2\pi i \), as if using Cauchy's integral formula on a singularity.
This uses \( \pi i \) instead of the correct value of 0 for an entire integrand.
This drops the coefficient \( m \) from the same mistaken residue-style computation.
This evaluates \( g(1) \) as if that were the integral, ignoring the theorem entirely.
